angle: I thought I was investing. I was just buying companies I could explain at dinner parties.
caption: Three years of investing before I realised I was just buying companies I could explain at dinner parties. What was the first thing you ever invested in? Drop it below — genuinely curious, zero judgment.
threads_text: I thought I was investing. I was just buying companies I could explain at dinner parties. Knew exactly what they did. Had no idea if they were mispriced. Three years and average returns to learn the difference. What was the first stock or asset you ever bought?

slides.main_text: I thought I was investing. **I was just buying companies I could explain at dinner parties.**
slides.sub_text: Three years and average returns to figure out the difference.
How long it took me to understand what an edge actually is
slides.stat_label: years investing before I got this
slides.bubble: My portfolio looked like a TED Talk watchlist. Great companies. Average returns.

slides.main_text: **Knowing what a company does** is not the same as knowing it is mispriced.
slides.sub_text: I could explain the business model in two sentences. That felt like research. It was not research.
slides.bubble: Turns out everyone at the dinner party already knew too.

slides.main_text: The **clarity trap**: understanding a company does not equal conviction it will outperform.
slides.sub_text: A good business and a good investment are different questions. I kept confusing the first for the second.
slides.bubble: Bought Apple at a price that made complete sense and still underperformed the index.

slides.main_text: I was buying **stories**, not bets.
slides.sub_text: Stories feel safe. Bets require you to be specifically right about something the market is specifically wrong about.
slides.bubble: Much easier to say 'I believe in this company' than to say exactly what you think the market is missing.

slides.main_text: The question I should have been asking: **what do I believe that the market does not?**
slides.sub_text: If the answer is nothing, you are not making a bet. You are just paying market price for market returns.
slides.bubble: Still working on having a real answer to this. Most days I don't.

slides.main_text: Liking a company and **having an edge on a company** are not the same thing.
slides.sub_text: What was the first thing you ever invested in? Drop it below.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Mine was a Malaysian bank stock I bought because I had an account there. True story.

angle: Building as a non-technical person is hell mode. Here is what makes it easier.
caption:
Building as a non-technical person is hell mode. Here's the one habit that helps.

Never start with build. Explore first, real references and best practices. Then Plan, let Claude lay it out. Then Execute.

Skipping straight to build is how you end up debugging code you don't understand.

Do you plan before you build? Vote: always / sometimes / never
threads_text:
Building as a non-technical person is hell mode. One habit made it easier: never start with build.

Explore first, then Plan, then Execute. Skipping straight to code is how you end up debugging something you don't understand.

Do you plan before you build, or just wing it?

slides.main_text: Building as a non-technical person is hell mode. **One habit** made it survivable.
slides.sub_text: Not a tool. A sequence.
Steps, every single time, no matter how small the task
slides.stat_label: steps I never skip anymore
slides.bubble: Skipping straight to build is how I've wasted the most hours, by far.

slides.main_text: **Explore first.** Real references, real best practices.
slides.sub_text: If I do not understand it, Claude does not either. I ask it to explain the codebase back to me before anything gets touched.
slides.bubble: Feels slow. Saves hours later.

slides.main_text: **Then Plan.** Let Claude lay it all out first.
slides.sub_text: I read the plan like a spec, not a suggestion.
slides.bubble: Half my edits happen here, before a single line is written.

slides.main_text: **Then Execute.** Only after the first two.
slides.sub_text: By the time we get here, there are barely any surprises left.
slides.bubble: This is the boring part now. That is the whole point.

slides.main_text: **Explore. Plan. Execute.** In that order, every time.
slides.sub_text: Skipping straight to build is still the mistake I have to actively resist.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Do you plan before you build, or just wing it?

angle: MBB taught me one skill that transfers to startups. Everything else I had to unlearn.
caption:
MBB taught me one skill that actually transfers to startups. The rest I had to unlearn, and some of it took embarrassingly long.

Swipe to see what stuck and what didn't.

What's the one skill from your current job that would help in a startup?
threads_text:
One skill from consulting transfers to startups. Everything else I had to unlearn.

The dangerous one: mistaking analysis for action. I was very good at being thorough. Startups don't reward thorough. They reward moving.

What's the one skill from your job that would actually help in a startup?

slides.main_text: MBB taught me **one skill** that transfers to startups.
slides.sub_text: Everything else I had to unlearn, and some of it took longer than I expected.
Number of skills from 5 years of consulting I use every single day building this
slides.stat_label: just one skill made the cut
slides.bubble: I thought it would be more. It is not more.

slides.main_text: **Structuring the problem** breaks into three things.
slides.sub_text: Ask the right question. Set the right goal. Get the right capability. I used to skip straight to solving.
slides.bubble: I spent years being the person with the smart-sounding answer. Still unlearning that.

slides.main_text: First: **asking the right question.**
slides.sub_text: A right question is specific enough to be wrong. Not "how do we grow," but "why did the last 10 people say no."
slides.bubble: I used to ask questions that sounded smart in a room and meant nothing once I sat down alone to actually build.

slides.main_text: Second: **setting the right goal.**
slides.sub_text: A right goal is a number you can hit or miss by Friday, not a five-year vision. Consulting trained me to set goals that sound good in a deck.
slides.bubble: My first goal for this was "grow steadily." That is not a goal. That is a wish.

slides.main_text: Third: **getting the right capability.**
slides.sub_text: In plain terms: do you actually have someone who can do the thing, not someone who can explain why it's hard.
slides.bubble: I cannot design, cannot really code, cannot sell yet. Some days that gap feels bigger than the idea itself.

slides.main_text: The dangerous habit: **mistaking analysis for action**
slides.sub_text: Consulting pays you to be right, not fast. Startups reward people who move; being thorough while the runway burns is just expensive thinking.
slides.bubble: I have 8,471 agents scraped and zero paying customers. Analysis did not cause that. But it did not help either.

slides.main_text: **One skill transfers. The rest is dead weight.**
slides.sub_text: Figure out which habits your current job is rewarding that a startup would punish.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Still working on this myself. 100 cold emails a day right now, hoping structure beats inertia.

angle: After trying everything, I use one investing principle. It is boring. It works.
caption: I don't DCA into an index fund — I bet on sectors right before they bottleneck: space, AI infra, semiconductors, biomed. Concentrated, not diversified, and I don't always time it right. What's your actual investing style? Vote: index funds / sector bets / individual stocks / not investing yet
threads_text: I don't dollar-cost-average into an index fund. I bet on sectors right before they bottleneck — space, AI infrastructure, semiconductors, biomed. Concentrated, not diversified. Higher risk, and I don't always get the timing right. What's your actual investing style?

slides.main_text: I don't dollar-cost-average into an index fund. **I bet on sectors right before they bottleneck.**
slides.sub_text: Space, AI infrastructure, semiconductors, biomed. Concentrated, not diversified.
Sectors I actually invest in, not the whole market
slides.stat_label: high-growth sectors, concentrated not diversified
slides.bubble: I have strong opinions about supply chains most people have never thought about once.

slides.main_text: The thesis: **find the bottleneck before everyone else does.**
slides.sub_text: Every high-growth sector eventually hits a capacity constraint. That is usually where the value sits.
slides.bubble: Sounds smart until you are three months early and the position is just red.

slides.main_text: **Concentrated,** not diversified.
slides.sub_text: Four sectors, not four hundred stocks. I would rather be wrong with conviction than right by accident.
slides.bubble: My portfolio would give a financial advisor a small heart attack.

slides.main_text: This is **not** the boring, safe version of investing.
slides.sub_text: Higher risk, higher variance, and I do not always get the timing right.
slides.bubble: I have eaten some real losses being early. That part does not make it into the highlight reel.

slides.main_text: **Conviction, not comfort.** That is the actual strategy.
slides.sub_text: It will not work for everyone. It is the only version of investing I have ever been able to stick with.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Ask me again in ten years whether this was smart or just lucky.

angle: I have been building alone for months and nobody warned me about this part.
caption: Building alone is fine until it isn't. Solo founders — what is the one thing nobody warned you about? Drop it below.
threads_text: Nobody talks about this part of building alone. Not the workload. The silence. You make a call, sit with it, and there is literally no one to tell you if you got it wrong. Shipping solo with a full-time job and zero paying customers is a very specific kind of quiet. What is the hardest part nobody mentions?

slides.main_text: I have been building alone for months and **nobody warned me about this part.**
slides.sub_text: It is not the work. It is the silence.
People on the team who can tell you if you are making the right call.
slides.stat_label: that is the whole team
slides.bubble: I texted my friend about a product decision at 11pm. He replied 'sounds good bro.' That was my feedback loop.

slides.main_text: There is **no standup. No Slack. No one to share a small win with.**
slides.sub_text: You hit a milestone and just close the laptop. The win still happened, it just evaporated.
slides.bubble: I told a friend. He said 'sounds good bro' and moved on.

slides.main_text: I made a **decision I am still not sure about.** Had to live with it alone.
slides.sub_text: No one to stress-test it. No one to talk me out of it or into it.
slides.bubble: I did eventually make a call. It might be wrong. I will probably find out in six weeks when the numbers tell me.

slides.main_text: Loneliness is not really about **people.** It is about **no one catching your blind spots.**
slides.sub_text: You can be surrounded and still building in a fog.
slides.bubble: A co-founder would push back. A friend replies 'sounds good bro.' These are not the same thing and I keep pretending they are.

slides.main_text: **Therapy is cheaper than a co-founder.** Barely.
slides.sub_text: At least someone is professionally obligated to engage with your decisions.
slides.bubble: Half joking. Maybe less than half.

slides.main_text: Building alone does not cost you work. It costs you **blind spots.**
slides.sub_text: Fill in the blank: nobody warned me building alone would ___.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Still figuring this one out. No clean lesson here.

angle: Consulting trained me to be excellent at things that do not matter. Startups cured me of this. Slowly.
caption:
MBB trained me to make things look good. Startups trained me to make things work. These are not the same skill.

I spent years getting very good at the wrong things. The transition was humbling.

What is something you optimised perfectly that turned out not to matter?
threads_text:
MBB trained me to make things look good. Startups trained me to make things work. These are not the same skill.

I spent years getting good at the wrong things.

What is something you optimised perfectly that turned out not to matter?

slides.main_text: Consulting trained me to be **excellent at things that do not matter.**
slides.sub_text: Startups cured me of this. Slower than I expected.
Hours I spent on slide decks that changed nothing at the client
slides.stat_label: hours on slides, 80% of everything I did for 5 years
slides.bubble: That is not an exaggeration. I did the math and immediately regretted it.

slides.main_text: The client decided in **10 minutes.** The deck took 3 weeks.
slides.sub_text: Pixel-perfect transitions, consistent font hierarchy, an appendix nobody opened. The decision was made before we even walked in.
slides.bubble: I once spent two days aligning boxes on a slide. Two days.

slides.main_text: The **consultant trap:** effort feels like impact because it looks like impact.
slides.sub_text: Hours in, polished output, rigorous process. But output is not outcome. Consulting rewards performing work; startups punish it.
slides.bubble: Nobody claps for your framework if the thing does not ship.

slides.main_text: A **working feature** beats a perfect plan. Every single time.
slides.sub_text: It is live. Zero paying customers, two working features. More real than any deck I ever made.
slides.bubble: My first pitch deck had 14 slides. what I built had 2 working features. I had the order wrong.

slides.main_text: The moment I realised I was **optimising for the wrong thing.**
slides.sub_text: I caught myself rewriting copy on a landing page nobody had visited. Classic consultant move: polish the thing, avoid the scary one.
slides.bubble: The scary thing was sending the cold email. The copy was fine.

slides.main_text: **Excellent execution** on the wrong thing is still the wrong thing.
slides.sub_text: The skill is not doing things well. It is picking the right things to do at all.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: What did you optimise perfectly that turned out not to matter?

angle: A startup taught me something consulting never did. It was not what I expected.
caption:
A startup taught me what 'fast' actually means. Consulting had it backwards for 5 years.

MBB taught me tight deadlines and late-night decks. Turns out that was not fast.

At a startup, fast means something else. Seeing it up close, I felt lied to.

What is the biggest speed difference between big and small company?
threads_text:
A startup taught me what 'fast' actually means. Consulting had it backwards for 5 years.

In consulting, moving fast meant finishing the deck before the client walked in.

At a startup, moving fast means shipping before someone else does.

Very different stakes. Very different clock.

What is the biggest speed gap you have noticed between big and small company?

slides.main_text: A startup taught me what **'fast'** actually means. Consulting had it backwards for 5 years.
slides.sub_text: 48 hours from idea to shipped. Consulting called weeks fast.
Hours from idea to shipped at a startup vs weeks in consulting
slides.stat_label: hours
slides.bubble: I once spent 3 weeks on a deliverable a startup would have shipped, iterated, and shipped again.

slides.main_text: In consulting, **moving fast** means finishing the deck before the client meeting.
slides.sub_text: The urgency is real. The output is a PowerPoint, and nobody is shipping anything.
slides.bubble: Slides do not go live. Code does.

slides.main_text: At a startup, **moving fast** means shipping before someone else does.
slides.sub_text: The competitor is not in the room. They are already building.
slides.bubble: The clock is always running. You just cannot see it.

slides.main_text: First time I saw a feature go from **Slack message to live** in two days.
slides.sub_text: I genuinely did not know that was possible.
slides.bubble: I asked if that was normal. Everyone looked at me like I was slow.

slides.main_text: **Speed is a competitive advantage.** Slowness is a silent killer.
slides.sub_text: You do not feel it until someone else is already three versions ahead of you.
slides.bubble: It is live. Zero paying customers. But it is live. That part matters.

slides.main_text: Consulting taught me **structure.** A startup taught me to **ship the thing.**
slides.sub_text: Both matter. But only one of them compounds.
slides.follow_cta: Follow to know what happens next or save this for later.
slides.bubble: What is the biggest speed difference you have noticed between big company and small company?

angle: I am cold emailing 8,471 strangers. Automatically. Here is what I built.
caption: Built a cold email machine for 8,471 strangers. No agency, no budget, no clue if it'll work. What's your cold email open rate? Drop it below — I want to know if I'm embarrassing myself.
threads_text: 100 cold emails go out at 1pm every day, automatically, to rental agents I scraped myself. 3-sequence flow, replies hit a real inbox I check every morning. Built it while employed full-time. Zero paying customers so far. What's your cold email open rate? Genuinely asking.

slides.main_text: I am cold emailing **8,471 strangers.** Automatically. Here is what I built.
slides.sub_text: Automated while working full-time, with no agency and no budget.
Not a blast. Not random. The same time, every day.
slides.stat_label: cold emails sent automatically at 1pm, every single day
slides.bubble: I feel like a spammer. I also feel like this is the only option I have right now.

slides.main_text: I built **the scraper, sequences, and send logic** myself.
slides.sub_text: No agency. No contractor. Just me, Claude, and a lot of trial and error. Non-coder. Vibe coding. It works, somehow.
slides.bubble: I cannot explain how half of it works. It just does.

slides.main_text: **3 sequences.** Day 1 intro. Day 4 follow-up. Day 8 final.
slides.sub_text: Each one is short. No fluff. I wrote them the way I'd want to be emailed.
slides.bubble: Day 8 email is the one I'd be most embarrassed for someone to screenshot.

slides.main_text: **Reply-to goes to a real inbox** I check every morning.
slides.sub_text: If someone writes back, it lands with me directly. No automation handles the reply. That part is still just me.
slides.bubble: Zero replies so far. Which is its own kind of feedback.

slides.main_text: This is what **distribution looks like with no budget and no audience.**
slides.sub_text: You scrape. You write. You send. You wait. It is live. Zero paying customers.
slides.bubble: The gap between 'built it' and 'anyone cares' is larger than I expected.

slides.main_text: **Cold outreach is not glamorous.** It is just work nobody is watching yet.
slides.sub_text: Still running. Still not sure this is the right call.

slides.bubble: If you have done cold email before, drop your open rate. I need a benchmark.

angle: I showed my product to 4 people before it was ready. Two said they would pay. Neither has.
caption: Showed it before it was ready. Two people said they'd pay. Neither has. Have you ever shown something unfinished and had someone say they'd buy it — did they actually? Drop a yes or no below.
threads_text: Showed it to 4 agents before it was ready. Broken layout, confusing onboarding, three features that half-worked. Two said they'd pay for it. Neither has. 'I would pay for this' and actually paying are completely different sentences. Still figuring out which one to trust.

slides.main_text: I showed **it** to 4 people before it was ready. **Two said they would pay. Neither has.**
slides.sub_text: Broken layout, half-finished features, and I showed it anyway.
Agents I showed it to before it was ready
slides.stat_label: real humans. real feedback. zero paying customers.
slides.bubble: I polished one screen for two hours before the demo. They spent 40 seconds on it.

slides.main_text: It was **not ready**. I showed it anyway.
slides.sub_text: Broken mobile layout. Confusing onboarding. Three features that half-worked. Waiting for perfect is just waiting forever with extra steps.
slides.bubble: I kept thinking one more fix and then I'll show it. That was three months ago.

slides.main_text: The feedback was useful. The **silence after** was more useful.
slides.sub_text: People tell you what they think when you're in the room. What they do when you leave tells you the truth.

slides.main_text: **"I would pay for this"** and actually paying are completely different things.
slides.sub_text: I know that intellectually. I believed them anyway.
slides.bubble: Still zero paying customers. The cold email campaign is running. We'll see.

slides.main_text: Show it **before you're ready**. But don't count the compliments.
slides.sub_text: The only signal that matters is someone typing in their card details.

angle: What working remotely for an overseas startup is actually like
caption: Everyone romanticises working remotely for an overseas startup. Here is what it actually looks like.
threads_text: Working remotely for an overseas startup sounds cool until you are on a late-night Zoom while your friends are out. The pay is decent. The timezone is not. You are always half a day behind everyone who matters. Still beats a lot of alternatives though.

slides.main_text: Working remotely for an **overseas startup** sounds like the dream.
slides.sub_text: Here is what nobody tells you about it.

slides.title: The time tax
slides.sub_text: Hours between me and the rest of the team, every single day.
slides.stat_label: behind headquarters. Every single day.
slides.bubble: My most productive hours are when my boss is asleep. Make of that what you will.

slides.title: The schedule
slides.main_text: Your **mornings are free.** Your evenings are not.
slides.sub_text: All the real meetings happen at the end of your day, so you learn to protect your mornings like they are the only hours you actually own.
slides.bubble: I have perfected the art of looking alert on camera late at night.

slides.title: The math
slides.main_text: The **pay gap** is real and it works in your favour.
slides.sub_text: Headquarters salary, local cost of living. The math is genuinely good, and this is the one part that lives up to the hype.
slides.bubble: Not going to pretend this is not a big part of why most people do it.

slides.title: The downside
slides.main_text: But you are always **half a day behind** everyone who matters.
slides.sub_text: Decisions get made while you sleep, and you catch up in the morning to find the landscape has already shifted.
slides.bubble: Nothing like waking up to 47 Slack messages and a decision that affects your whole week.

slides.title: The opportunity
slides.main_text: So I started building in the **pockets of time I actually own.**
slides.sub_text: 8,471 cold emails out to potential customers. 100 a day, automated. Zero paying customers so far. But the mornings are mine.
slides.bubble: Running a side project while the day job sleeps. Very normal behaviour.

slides.title: The takeaway
slides.main_text: The **timezone gap** is the tax. The free mornings are the opportunity.
slides.sub_text: Still figuring out if I am playing it right.
slides.bubble: Ask me again once the side project actually makes money.

angle: Day 1 — Navigating career: left MBB after 5 years
caption:

threads_text: I left a $60k/month job at MBB. Nobody told me what comes next.

slides.main_text: I left a **$60k/month job** at MBB after 5 years.
slides.sub_text: Probably one of the dumbest decisions I have ever made in my life.

slides.title: The Exit
slides.sub_text: Everyone asked why I was leaving. Two years more and I'll get promoted to leadership. It's a stupid decision.

slides.title: The Reality
slides.main_text: The meetings you **complained about** was also routine comfort.
slides.sub_text: The structure, the meetings, the drill, everything was so organized.
slides.bubble: Week one. No meetings, no deliverables, no one to report to. I did not know what to do with myself.

slides.title: The Loss
slides.main_text: Here is what nobody **puts on the list.**
slides.sub_text: The title. The peers. The salary. And knowing exactly what you were supposed to do every day.
slides.bubble: I feel disconnected. I feel like I lost a part of my identity. I don't know who I am.

slides.title: The Takeaway
slides.main_text: Leaving is not the **brave part.** Figuring out who you are after is.
slides.sub_text: Still working on it. I want to share to the world as it happens.

angle: Consulting actively trains you to be a terrible founder. I am proving this in real time.
caption:

What habit from your job would hurt you as a founder?
threads_text:

What habit from your job would hurt you as a founder?

slides.main_text: Consulting actively trains you to be **a terrible founder.**
slides.sub_text: Three reasons why, and I have lived all of them.
Founder habits that consulting actively trains out of you
slides.stat_label: and I am currently 0 for 3
slides.bubble: I spent a week researching whether to use Notion or Obsidian for notes. A real founder would have picked one in 5 minutes.

slides.main_text: Reason 1: You are **wired to reduce risk,** not take it.
slides.sub_text: Consulting rewards you for spotting what can go wrong and building a slide about it. Founding rewards you for shipping anyway. These are opposite instincts.
slides.bubble: I have a 3-tab spreadsheet analysing cold email risk. I should have just sent the emails two weeks earlier.

slides.main_text: Reason 2: You are used to **clients defining the problem.**
slides.sub_text: In consulting, someone hands you the question. As a founder, choosing the right problem is the whole job.
slides.bubble: I kept waiting for my users to tell me exactly what to build. They do not know either.

slides.main_text: Reason 3: Optimised for **looking right,** not being right.
slides.sub_text: MBB trains you to never walk into a room without the answer already locked. Founding means being publicly wrong, repeatedly, and updating fast. The deck is never done.
slides.bubble: It's live. Zero paying customers. This post exists. Make of that what you will.

slides.main_text: The caveat: **some of us recover.**
slides.sub_text: I am trying. Slowly. With 8,471 scraped agents and 100 cold emails going out per day as proof I eventually moved.

angle: Everyone says quit and go all in. Here is why I have not. It is not fear.

slides.bubble: 8 months sounds okay until month 3 when you are still at zero customers.

slides.main_text: **Runway is not a vibe.** It is a number.
slides.sub_text: Savings divided by monthly burn. That is it. Mine does not survive long enough to wait out a slow B2B sales cycle.
slides.bubble: I did the spreadsheet. It was not encouraging.

slides.main_text: **Going all in** is romantic. Going broke is not.
slides.sub_text: Every founder who says 'just commit' had savings, a partner's income, or a safety net they are not mentioning.
slides.bubble: I have seen the LinkedIn posts. Nobody mentions the safety net.

slides.main_text: The job **funds the experiment.**
slides.sub_text: The experiment might one day make the job optional. Right now it is live, 8,471 agents are in the pipeline, and I have zero paying customers. The job stays.
slides.bubble: This is not settling. This is not running out of money before getting an answer.

slides.main_text: I will quit when I hit a **specific revenue number.** Not a feeling.
slides.sub_text: Not when it feels right. Not when I feel confident enough. A milestone. A real one. Written down.
slides.bubble: Feelings are not a financial plan.

slides.main_text: **Going all in** only makes sense when the math says you can survive being wrong.
slides.sub_text: Until then, the job is not the enemy. It is the bet hedge.

slides.main_text: The **GPA filter** is real. But it is not the only door.

slides.bubble: Most people do not ask because they assume the answer is no. The answer is sometimes yes.

slides.main_text: Case prep was **8 weeks of my life.** Some of it was useful.
slides.sub_text: Case in Point: read once, forgotten. What worked was practising out loud, daily, with a real person.
slides.bubble: I practised cases with a stranger from LinkedIn for 6 weeks. He got in too. We have never met in person.

slides.main_text: When the call came, I did not know if I was **relieved or terrified.**
slides.sub_text: Both, probably. Mostly terrified.
slides.bubble: I said thank you, hung up, and sat very still for about five minutes.

slides.main_text: The application process rewards **people who refuse to disqualify themselves.**
slides.sub_text: Your GPA is a filter. It is not a verdict.

angle: The anxiety inside MBB is not what you think. It is quieter. And harder to shake.
caption:
The anxiety inside MBB is not what people imagine.

It's not the 80-hour weeks or the decks. It's something quieter, harder to name.

Nobody in the building brings it up first.

Have you ever felt like you didn't belong somewhere everyone else seemed fine? Vote: yes, often / yes, once / never felt that way
threads_text: The anxiety inside MBB is not what you think. It is not the work. It is sitting in a room full of people who all seem completely fine, wondering if you are the only one who is not. Nobody says anything. So you assume you are the problem. Have you ever felt like that somewhere?

slides.main_text: The anxiety inside MBB is **not what you think.**
slides.sub_text: Not the kind of stress people imagine from the outside. The kind nobody admits to.
Times I seriously considered leaving in year one
slides.stat_label: and both times I stayed because I was too scared to admit it was not working
slides.bubble: I googled 'is it normal to feel like you do not belong at MBB' at 1am. The results were not comforting.

slides.main_text: Everyone around you is **exceptional.** That becomes the baseline.
slides.sub_text: When exceptional is average, you are not competing with a bar. You are competing with a room. It does not feel inspiring. It feels like a slow drip of not enough.
slides.bubble: You stop celebrating your own wins because someone next to you did it faster.

slides.main_text: The fear is not **failing the work.** It is failing in front of them.
slides.sub_text: You can redo a slide. You cannot undo the moment a partner watched you not know the answer.
slides.bubble: I rehearsed answers to questions nobody asked just so I would never look blank.

slides.main_text: **Sunday nights** had a specific texture.
slides.sub_text: Not dread about Monday. Dread starting sometime around 4pm Sunday that you could not explain to anyone outside the firm.
slides.bubble: My friends thought I was being dramatic. I was not being dramatic.

slides.main_text: The culture of **not showing weakness** makes all of it worse.
slides.sub_text: Nobody talks about this out loud. So you assume everyone else is fine and you are the exception. You are not the exception. But you will not find that out until years later.
slides.bubble: The irony is everyone is doing the same performance and nobody is breaking first.

slides.main_text: The hardest part was not the work. It was **pretending it was.**
slides.sub_text: If you felt out of place somewhere everyone else looked fine, it probably was not just you.

angle: The real setup behind Claude Code: MCP, Playwright, shadcn, 21st.dev, and Context7, explained in plain terms.
caption:

MCP is the plug that lets Claude reach real tools, like a USB port for AI. Playwright clicks through a real browser like a person. Shadcn and 21st.dev build the interface. Context7 keeps the docs current.

threads_text:
I spent too much time prompting a bad tool.

MCP is the plug that lets Claude reach real tools, like a USB port for AI. Playwright clicks through a real browser like a person. Shadcn and 21st.dev build the interface. Context7 keeps the docs current.

4 skills later, grateful for all of them.

Which one would you use first?

slides.main_text: I spent **too much time** prompting a bad tool.
slides.sub_text: Here is the setup that actually changed how I build.

slides.title: Four
slides.sub_text: The tools covered in this post.
slides.stat_label: skills that changed how I build
slides.bubble: Each one replaces hours of prompting a tool that was not built for this.

slides.title: What MCP actually is
slides.main_text: **MCP** is the plug that lets Claude reach real tools.
slides.sub_text: Think of it like a USB port for AI. One standard plug, any tool on the other end, instead of a custom setup for every single one.
slides.bubble: This is the difference between an assistant that talks and one that actually does the work.

slides.title: What Playwright does
slides.main_text: **Playwright** lets Claude open a real browser.
slides.sub_text: It clicks buttons, fills forms, resizes the screen, and takes screenshots, exactly like a real visitor would.
slides.bubble: It once caught a button rendering invisible behind another element. I would never have found that just reading code.

slides.title: Where the UI comes from
slides.main_text: **Shadcn** and **21st.dev** build the interface.
slides.sub_text: Shadcn gives Claude ready made, accessible components it can copy straight into the project, not install as a dependency. 21st.dev generates new ones from a plain text description.
slides.bubble: I do not design a single button myself anymore.

slides.title: Context7 keeps Claude current
slides.main_text: **Context7** stops Claude from guessing outdated docs.
slides.sub_text: It pulls the real, current documentation for whatever tool I am using, instead of relying on what Claude was trained on months ago.
slides.bubble: AI tools change every week. This is how Claude keeps up instead of falling behind.

slides.main_text: There are **more skills** I have not covered yet.
slides.sub_text: I will go through the rest next.

slides.bubble: Note to self: the posts that feel too personal are usually the ones worth keeping.

slides.main_text: The **honest take** that made people uncomfortable also made people DM me.
slides.sub_text: Not angry DMs. More like 'I've never seen someone say that out loud' DMs.
slides.bubble: Which is its own kind of uncomfortable, honestly.

slides.main_text: Three weeks taught me **who is actually following.**
slides.sub_text: Not the audience I assumed. More people mid-career, more people outside Malaysia than I expected. I built a mental picture of my reader that was mostly wrong.
slides.bubble: Still recalibrating. Not sure I've got it right yet.

slides.main_text: **What comes next** depends on what you actually want to read.
slides.sub_text: I have three directions I can go: building in public, managing finances, or life outside work. I'll follow the votes. No point guessing again.
slides.bubble: My guesses have a 0% hit rate so far this month.

slides.main_text: Three weeks in. Still **figuring out** who I'm writing for.
slides.sub_text: Which is probably the most honest career content I could post right now.

angle: What building something on the side actually did to my relationship. Not the version I posted about.
caption: Building something on the side has a cost nobody talks about. My fiancée absorbs it too. If you're building something, has it changed a relationship? Vote in the comments: yes a lot / a little / not yet / I'm not building anything.
threads_text: Building kakisewa does something to a relationship nobody warns you about. Not the dramatic version. The quiet version — nights that disappear, attention that drifts, a person next to you who never asked to compete with a laptop. Has it changed a relationship for you?

slides.main_text:
What building **kakisewa** actually did to my relationship.

Not the version I posted about.
slides.sub_text: My fiancée did not sign up to compete with a laptop every night.
Hours a week that quietly shifted from us to the screen
slides.stat_label: hours a week that went from us to the laptop
slides.bubble: I did not track it until she pointed it out. Then I could not un-see it.

slides.main_text: The nights did not **disappear all at once.**
slides.sub_text: It was one hour here, one there. Then it was just how evenings worked.
slides.sub_text_2: Neither of us decided that. It just happened.
slides.bubble: The worst part is I thought I was being efficient. She thought I was checked out.

slides.main_text: She never asked me to **stop.**
slides.sub_text: She asked me to tell her when I was starting, so it stopped being a surprise every night.
slides.bubble: That one sentence fixed more than any schedule I tried to build.

slides.main_text: The compromise that **stuck** and the one that did not.
slides.sub_text: Telling her when I was starting: stuck. Fixed laptop-off times: lasted about a week.
slides.sub_text_2: Turns out rules I set for myself are easier to break than a heads-up to another person.
slides.bubble: Accountability works better when someone else is holding it.

slides.main_text: Building alone does not mean **living alone.**
slides.sub_text: Someone else absorbs the cost of what you are building.
slides.sub_text_2: Zero paying customers so far. She is still here. That is not nothing.
slides.bubble: I think about that more than I probably let on.

slides.main_text: **kakisewa** is mine. The cost is shared.
slides.sub_text: Worth naming that out loud, not just optimizing around it.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Still figuring out the balance. She would probably agree with that.

angle: One year into the startup job. Here is what actually surprised me. Not what I expected going in.
caption: One year in, and nobody has ever once asked me for a slide. That is what surprised me most. If you have made a similar jump, vote in the comments: what hit hardest in year one? The pace / the ambiguity / the lack of structure / something else entirely.
threads_text:
One year in, and nobody has ever once asked me for a slide. Startup pace is not what I thought it was.

What hit hardest was not the speed — nobody wanted a structured framework. They wanted a decision.

What surprised you most in year one after a big job switch?

slides.main_text: One year in, and nobody has ever once asked me for a **slide.**
slides.sub_text: Startup pace is not what I thought it was from the consulting side.
Months in before it stopped feeling like a different planet
slides.stat_label: months until it felt normal
slides.bubble: I am not sure it fully has yet, if I am being honest.

slides.main_text: What I thought startup life would be: **consulting, but faster.**
slides.sub_text: Tight decks. Clear problem statements. Smart people debating options. That is not what I walked into.
slides.bubble: I genuinely showed up expecting a structured kickoff. There was no kickoff.

slides.main_text: Month one surprise: **nobody wanted the framework.**
slides.sub_text: They wanted a call made, today, with incomplete information. MBB trained me to get comfortable before deciding. That had to go.
slides.bubble: I still catch myself opening a blank slide to structure a problem. Nobody here has ever asked for a slide.

slides.main_text: The skill MBB did not teach me: **tolerance for irreversible ambiguity.**
slides.sub_text: Consulting ambiguity has a deadline. Someone always clarifies the brief eventually. Startup ambiguity just lives there. You act anyway.
slides.bubble: A year in and I still find this uncomfortable. I just complain about it less.

slides.main_text: What I would tell my consulting self: **the job will not validate you the way the project did.**
slides.sub_text: No end-of-engagement wrap-up. No client sign-off. No clear 'we did it.'. You have to get okay with outputs that just keep moving.
slides.bubble: I thought I was ready for that. I was not ready for that.

slides.main_text: A year in, the biggest shift is this: **I stopped waiting to feel ready before I moved.**
slides.sub_text: Still working on it. But the gap between consulting me and now is real.
slides.follow_cta: Follow to know what happens next or save this for later.
slides.bubble: What hit hardest for you in year one? Vote above.

angle: The consulting salary is designed to make you comfortable enough to stay. Here is the trap.
caption: The consulting salary felt like success. My savings rate told a different story. What's yours right now — drop the actual % in the comments.
threads_text: The consulting salary is designed to make you comfortable enough to stay. High income, invisible lifestyle creep, and a savings rate you stop checking because you don't want to see it. I thought I was saving well. Then I ran the numbers. What's your actual savings rate right now — rough % is fine, be honest.

slides.main_text: The consulting salary is **designed to make you comfortable enough to stay.**
slides.sub_text: High income that quietly inflates your lifestyle until leaving feels unthinkable.
The share of my consulting income that quietly disappeared into lifestyle
slides.stat_label: of my income that went to lifestyle, not savings
slides.bubble: I thought I was saving well. Then I calculated my actual savings rate. It was not what I thought.

slides.main_text: Lifestyle inflation is **invisible while it's happening.**
slides.sub_text: You don't decide to spend more. You just stop noticing what things cost.
slides.sub_text_2: It only becomes visible when the income stops.
slides.bubble: The hotel upgrade. The cab instead of the train. It felt like nothing each time.

slides.main_text: A high salary makes you **feel wealthy.** Your savings rate is the honest number.
slides.sub_text: Income is just the top line. What you keep is what actually matters.
slides.bubble: I was optimizing my portfolio while my savings rate quietly embarrassed me.

slides.main_text: Things I spent on in consulting that I **do not miss at all:**
slides.sub_text: Expensive lunches because the day was long. Clothes for a version of myself I was performing.
slides.sub_text_2: Most of it was friction relief, not enjoyment.
slides.bubble: I genuinely cannot tell you what I bought. That's the problem.

slides.main_text: First month my salary dropped: **the cuts were not what I planned.**
slides.sub_text: I thought I'd cut the big stuff. I actually cut the small daily things first.
slides.sub_text_2: The things I thought were non-negotiable mostly were.
slides.bubble: Turns out I had strong opinions about my expenses until I had to act on them.

slides.main_text: The salary wasn't the problem. The **invisible floor it built** was.
slides.sub_text: Once you know your real savings rate, you can't unknow it. Go check yours.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Drop your actual savings rate % in the comments. Rough estimate counts. I'll go first.

angle: The friendships that survived 5 years of a job that ate my calendar. Fewer than I expected.
caption: 5 years of MBB. Here is what it actually cost me in friendships. Has a demanding job ever cost you a friendship you wish you had kept? Vote in the comments: yes, lost one / yes, several / no, they held / not sure yet.
threads_text: Five years of a demanding job. Here is what happened to my friendships. Not a dramatic falling out. Just cancelled plans, repeated enough times, until the invites stopped coming. Has a demanding job ever cost you a friendship you wish you had kept? Yes lost one / yes several / no they held / not sure yet

slides.main_text: The friendships that survived 5 years of a job that ate my calendar. **Fewer than I expected.**
slides.sub_text: Not because anyone did anything wrong. Just distance, repeated enough times.
Close friends from before MBB I actually still talk to weekly
slides.stat_label: That number got smaller every year I stayed in the job
slides.bubble: I cancelled on the same friend three times in one month once. He stopped asking. I do not blame him.

slides.main_text: The group did not **disappear**. It just quietly thinned.
slides.sub_text: No fight. No falling out. Just enough cancelled plans that eventually nobody bothered to schedule one.
slides.bubble: You do not notice it happening. Then one day you do.

slides.main_text: The ones who stuck were not the ones who **tried harder**.
slides.sub_text: They were the ones whose lives happened to run on the same schedule as my rare free windows.
slides.sub_text_2: Proximity to timing, not effort. That is an uncomfortable thing to admit.
slides.bubble: Loyalty matters. But so does being available at the right moment, and I rarely was.

slides.main_text: Some friendships only existed because of a **shared job**.
slides.sub_text: The work was the glue. When I left, I found out pretty fast which ones were actually about me.
slides.bubble: Turns out complaining about the same client is a stronger bond than I realised. Until it is not.

slides.main_text: Leaving the job did not **automatically fix** anything.
slides.sub_text: I had to actually reach back out. Some conversations picked up fine. Some felt like meeting a stranger.
slides.sub_text_2: A few I have not managed to rebuild yet.
slides.bubble: Having free time and knowing how to use it socially are not the same thing. I am still figuring that out.

slides.main_text: The job did not ruin my friendships. But it **narrowed** them, and I let it.
slides.sub_text: I am more deliberate now. Not perfectly, but more.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Vote in the comments: yes lost one / yes several / no they held / not sure yet. Curious where people land on this.

angle: What I would tell my old MBB team about startups, now that I have actually been in one for a year.
caption:
12 months in. Here is what I would actually say to my old MBB team about startup life — not the polished version.

What do former colleagues get most wrong about your decision? Vote: scarier than it is / easier than it is / just the money / they don't ask
threads_text:
A year into startup life, former MBB colleagues keep asking the same questions. Most assumptions are wrong, but not always the direction you'd expect.

What do people get most wrong about why you left a prestigious job? Vote: scarier / easier / just the money / they never ask

slides.main_text: What I would tell my old MBB team about startups, **now that I have actually been in one for a year**
slides.sub_text: Not the LinkedIn version. The version I would actually say over coffee.
Former colleagues who have asked me some version of 'so... do you regret it yet'
slides.stat_label: people who deserve an honest answer, not a highlight reel
slides.bubble: My answer is still 'ask me again in a year.' It has been a year. I am still saying it.

slides.main_text: What they assume is true: **startups move faster**
slides.sub_text: Some things move faster. Decisions, yes. Feedback loops, yes. But ambiguity does not move fast. It just sits there and you have to live in it.
slides.bubble: Nobody warned me that unclear beats slow in terms of how exhausting it actually is.

slides.main_text: What they assume is false: **that the prestige safety net matters less once you leave**
slides.sub_text: It still opens doors. What I lost is the feeling the institution was backing me. Now it is just me.
slides.bubble: More freeing and more terrifying than I expected. Sometimes on the same Tuesday.

slides.main_text: What I miss and what I do not
slides.sub_text: Miss: smart rooms, peer pressure that made the work better, someone else owning the sales problem. Do not miss: the performance of busyness, the decks that changed nothing, the hierarchy I had to manage upward.
slides.bubble: I have 8,471 agents scraped and zero paying customers. I own the sales problem now. Fully.

slides.main_text: You are not leaving **safety for risk.** You are trading one risk for another.
slides.sub_text: MBB felt safe. It was betting everything on one employer's opinion of you.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: I do not know yet if I made the right call. My old colleagues do not want to hear that.

angle: One skill from consulting I use every single day at the startup job. Not the side project — the actual job.
caption: One consulting skill I actually use every day at the startup — not kakisewa, the real job. Which skill from a past role still follows you around? Vote in the comments: problem-structuring / communication / negotiation / something else.
threads_text:
One consulting skill I use every single day at the startup job. Not kakisewa — the actual job.

It's not slide-making. It's not frameworks. It's the ability to find the real question inside a messy one.

Which skill from a past job still follows you around? Problem-structuring / communication / negotiation / something else?

slides.main_text: One skill from consulting I use **every single day** at the startup job.
slides.sub_text: Not kakisewa. The actual job. And it's not what most people guess.
Rough count of how many times a week this skill saves me from a meeting going sideways
slides.stat_label: times a week I lean on it
slides.bubble: I did not expect 'consulting skill' and 'startup speed' to overlap this much.

slides.main_text: The skill: **distilling the actual question** out of a messy one.
slides.sub_text: Someone says 'we need to fix retention.' That is not a question. That is a feeling.
slides.sub_text_2: The question is usually three layers deeper and nobody has named it yet.
slides.bubble: Five years of this and it still takes effort. Just less embarrassing effort now.

slides.main_text: At a startup it shows up **constantly** — and faster than you expect.
slides.sub_text: Ambiguous asks, five-minute decisions, meetings where the agenda is also the problem.
slides.bubble: In my first startup meeting I tried to frame the problem the MBB way. Someone just said 'so what do we do.' Different game.

slides.main_text: What almost **did not transfer**: the pacing.
slides.sub_text: Consulting gave me the tool but trained me to use it slowly, with a deck, with buy-in loops.
slides.sub_text_2: I had to relearn how to do the same thing in about ninety seconds out loud.
slides.bubble: The skill transferred. The speed did not. Had to rebuild that part from scratch.

slides.main_text: Why **this** skill, out of everything consulting taught.
slides.sub_text: Slides fade. Frameworks date. But every job, every context, still has people solving the wrong problem.
slides.bubble: Honestly I thought client management would be the one. It is not. It is this.

slides.main_text: **Find the real question.** Everything else is just work on top of the wrong answer.
slides.sub_text: Which skill from a past job still follows you? Vote: problem-structuring / communication / negotiation / something else.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Still figuring out which consulting habits are assets and which ones just slow me down.

angle: I get 3 hours a day to build my startup. Here is the honest schedule.
caption: 3 hours a day to build. This is what the actual schedule looks like, including what I gave up to make it work. How many hours a day do you get for your own thing? Drop the number below.
threads_text: I get 3 hours a day to build. Not 3 focused, romantic, deep-work hours. 3 hours after a full day job, after dinner, after pretending I will stop at midnight. 8pm me and 6am me are completely different people. Took me a while to stop fighting that. How many hours do you actually get?

slides.main_text: I get **3 hours a day** to build. Here is the honest schedule.
slides.sub_text: The real breakdown, including what I gave up to make the hours work.
Not a goal. Not an average. The actual number most days.
slides.stat_label: hours per day I actually get to build
slides.bubble: I told myself I would stop at midnight. I stopped at 1:30am. I do this every few days.

slides.main_text: **Energy management** matters more than time management.
slides.sub_text: 8pm Howard is not the same as 6am Howard. I stopped pretending otherwise and picked my hours deliberately.
slides.bubble: Turns out writing logic at 11pm is fine. Writing copy at 11pm is a disaster.

slides.main_text: The **math only works** if your day job does not drain you completely.
slides.sub_text: Full-time startup job in Malaysia. Side project after hours. If work is a wreck, the 3 hours disappear. That is the fragile part I have not solved.
slides.bubble: Some weeks I get 3 hours. Some weeks I get 45 minutes and a headache.

slides.main_text: What I actually **gave up** to find the hours.
slides.sub_text: Netflix, most social plans, some sleep. I do not miss Netflix. I miss sleep.
slides.bubble: People keep asking when I will slow down. I do not have a good answer yet.

slides.main_text: 3 hours is enough. But only if you **protect them like they are the only ones you have.**
slides.sub_text: Because they are.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Still zero paying customers. Still showing up. Not sure if that is discipline or stubbornness.

angle: The default investment options are better than most investors think. Most people are ignoring free money.
caption: EPF and ASNB are the base I never touch. Everything else is where I take real risk — space, AI infra, semiconductors, biomed. Do you max your defaults before investing elsewhere? Vote: yes / no / did not know you could top up
threads_text: Most Malaysian investors are chasing higher returns while leaving guaranteed money sitting on the table. EPF and ASNB are not exciting. They are also not supposed to be. Boring base first, selective bets on top. Do you max your defaults before investing elsewhere? Yes / no / genuinely did not know you could top up.

slides.main_text: The default investment options are **better than most investors think.**
slides.sub_text: The boring defaults beat most people's active picks, and the data is not close.
The guaranteed return on one of the most overlooked default investments available to Malaysians
slides.stat_label: guaranteed return — no volatility, no stock-picking required
slides.bubble: This is the part of my portfolio that does not need my genius. That is the point.

slides.main_text: **EPF compounding** is real and most people underestimate it.
slides.sub_text: The dividend compounds on your entire balance, not just new contributions. Most people treat it as a deduction, not an asset. That framing is costing them.
slides.bubble: I did this too until I actually ran the numbers on what my balance would be at 55.

slides.main_text: **ASNB** is risk-free with a guaranteed return and most investors walk past it.
slides.sub_text: It has an allocation cap, which makes it annoying. Not a reason to ignore it.
slides.bubble: The cap being full is a reason to get in earlier, not a reason to give up.

slides.main_text: The mistake is **chasing higher returns** before locking in the guaranteed base.
slides.sub_text: People skip a guaranteed base to go hunt for more, and most do not find it.
slides.bubble: I have chased extra yield before locking in the base. It never actually paid off.

slides.main_text: How I actually think about it: **boring base first, selective bets on top.**
slides.sub_text: Max the guaranteed stuff first. What is left goes into the real bets — space, AI infra, semiconductors, biomed.
slides.bubble: It took me longer than I want to admit to stop treating it like one.

slides.main_text: **Free guaranteed returns** are what most people are too sophisticated to take.
slides.sub_text: Max the boring stuff first. You can still take your bets on top.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Still working out the right split. Have not fully figured it out either.

angle: The advice everyone gave me right after I left MBB. Some of it helped. Most of it missed the point.
caption:
Everyone had advice when I left MBB. Ex-consultants knew how to optimise the exit. Nobody told me what to do with myself after.

Best advice came from a friend with a normal 9-5 who did not know he was giving advice.

Vote: best advice came from a mentor / a friend outside my field / a stranger online / family
threads_text:
Everyone had advice when I left MBB. Ex-consultants told me how to optimise the exit. None told me what to do with myself after.

The best advice came from a friend with a normal job who had no idea it was even advice.

Where did your best career advice come from?

slides.main_text: The advice everyone gave me after I left MBB. **Some of it helped. Most of it missed the point.**
slides.sub_text: What actually helped came from people who had never worked a day in consulting.
Rough count of advice conversations I had in the weeks after leaving — the number I actually used:
slides.stat_label: pieces of advice I actually used
slides.bubble: The rest was well-meaning noise. Including some of my own plans.

slides.main_text: Ex-consultants gave me advice about **optimising the exit**
slides.sub_text: Which firm to join next. How to frame the gap. Which roles convert best from MBB. All useful if you know what you want. I did not know what I wanted.
slides.bubble: They answered a question I hadn't asked yet.

slides.main_text: Non-consultants gave me **simpler advice**
slides.sub_text: A friend with a normal 9-5 told me to just try things without needing them to work out immediately. He meant it casually. I wrote it down.
slides.bubble: He had no idea it was even good advice. That was probably why it landed.

slides.main_text: The one piece I **ignored** — and probably shouldn't have
slides.sub_text: Someone told me to take two months off before deciding anything. I took two weeks and called it enough. I don't think it was.
slides.bubble: Classic consultant move. Treat rest like a deliverable with a deadline.

slides.main_text: What I'd tell someone in my position **six months ago**
slides.sub_text: Stop asking people who optimised the same path you just left. Find someone who built something different and ask them what they were scared of.
slides.bubble: Still working this out myself, to be clear.

slides.main_text: The best advice didn't come from **experience in my field.** It came from distance from it.
slides.sub_text: Distance turned out to matter more than expertise.
slides.follow_cta: Save this for later or follow for what happens next.
slides.bubble: Still not sure I have taken enough of the good advice. Six months in.
